title: Shopping Cart & Order Management — System Design
description: Designing the cart-to-order path of an e-commerce platform: hold items in a cart, price them with promotions, then turn checkout into a durable order that coordinates payment, inventory, and fulfillment without losing money or overselling. The interesting problems are correctness under failure: a cart that survives sessions, pricing computed at the right moment, and a checkout that spans several independent services (a distributed transaction it can't truly have). We build from a naive cart toward a saga-based, idempotent, inventory-consistent order system.

functional: Add, update, and remove items in a cart.
Persist the cart across sessions and devices for a logged-in user.
Price the cart with taxes and promotions/coupons.
Check out: reserve inventory, take payment, and create an order.
Track order lifecycle (placed, paid, fulfilled, cancelled) and allow cancellation.
nonFunctional: Never oversell limited inventory; never charge without an order.
Idempotent checkout so retries don't double-order or double-charge.
Cart operations are fast and highly available (browsing must feel instant).
Order processing is durable and recoverable across service failures.
Pricing shown at checkout is honored (no surprise changes mid-flow).
Scale carts (huge, read/write heavy) independently from orders (fewer, correctness-critical).

capacity.label: Cart operations
capacity.note: Add/update/view is frequent and latency-sensitive; availability over strict consistency.

capacity.label: Checkouts
capacity.note: Far rarer than cart ops but correctness-critical (money + inventory).

capacity.label: Active carts
capacity.note: Most never convert; carts must be cheap to store and expire.

capacity.label: Inventory contention
capacity.note: Limited-stock or flash-sale items concentrate checkout contention.

capacity.label: Order durability
capacity.note: An accepted order survives service/region failure.

capacity.label: Read:write
capacity.note: Two very different workloads — split them.

api.description: Add or update a cart line item. Highly available; eventual consistency is fine.
Fetch the cart with current priced totals.
Convert a cart to an order: reserve inventory, charge payment, create the order. Idempotency-Key required.
api.response: 201 Created { "order_id": "o_5", "status": "placed" } — or 409 if an item is out of stock

api.description: Cancel an order; compensates (refund, release inventory) per its current state.

dataModel.name: carts
dataModel.note: A user's working set of items. Cheap, expiring, availability-first.

dataModel.name: orders
dataModel.note: The durable record of a placed order and its lifecycle state.

dataModel.name: inventory
dataModel.note: Authoritative stock per SKU, with reserved vs available, for oversell prevention.

dataModel.name: reservations
dataModel.note: Short-lived holds on inventory created at checkout; expire if checkout doesn't complete.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Dedupes retried checkouts; stores the first result.

patterns.name: Session vs persistent cart
patterns.what: Keep carts in a fast store keyed by user/session; merge guest carts on login.

patterns.name: Pricing at the right moment
patterns.what: Compute promotions/taxes at view and re-validate (and freeze) at checkout.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever prices/promotions change and must be honored consistently.

patterns.name: Saga / orchestration
patterns.what: Coordinate checkout across inventory, payment, and order via explicit steps with compensations.
patterns.whenToUse: A 'transaction' spanning independent services that can't share one ACID transaction.

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: Dedupe retried checkouts so a shopper isn't double-ordered or double-charged.
patterns.whenToUse: Money-moving operations over an unreliable network.

patterns.name: Inventory reservation + TTL
patterns.what: Hold stock during checkout; release if it doesn't complete in time.
patterns.whenToUse: Preventing oversell while a checkout is in flight.

patterns.name: Optimistic concurrency (CAS)
patterns.what: Decrement-if-available on stock so concurrent checkouts can't oversell.

patterns.name: Order state machine
patterns.what: Model the order as explicit states with guarded transitions and compensations.
patterns.whenToUse: Long-running order lifecycle across placement, payment, fulfillment, cancellation.

patterns.name: Transactional outbox
patterns.what: Emit order events atomically with the state change for fulfillment/notifications.
patterns.whenToUse: Reliable downstream events without dual writes.

patterns.name: Workload partitioning
patterns.what: Scale carts and orders as separate systems with different consistency needs.
patterns.whenToUse: When two sub-workloads have opposite priorities (availability vs correctness).

steps.title: 1. Naive Cart & Buy (the baseline)
steps.description: Start simple: a cart held in memory/session, and a 'buy' that decrements stock and records an order in one request. It works for one shopper; it loses carts on restart and, worse, treats a multi-service checkout (inventory + payment + order) as if it were one atomic step — which it can't be.
steps.decisionPrompt: Checkout must reserve stock, charge a card, and create an order across three services. What goes wrong treating that as one transaction?

concepts.definition: No single ACID transaction spans payment, inventory, and orders.
concepts.whyItMatters: A crash between steps leaves money moved but no order, or stock gone with no charge.

concepts.definition: Carts must survive sessions/restarts for logged-in users.
concepts.whyItMatters: An in-memory cart vanishing loses the shopper's intent.

talkingPoints: Name the two priorities: a durable, available cart and a correct multi-service checkout.
Flag that checkout is a saga, not a transaction.

traps.trap: Treating checkout as one atomic DB transaction across services
traps.why: Payment, inventory, and orders are separate systems; a failure mid-way loses money or stock with no order.
traps.instead: Model checkout as a saga with compensations and idempotency.

bottlenecks.issue: Single-request checkout can't be atomic across services and loses carts on restart.

strong: Spots that checkout spans services and can't be one transaction.
Separates cart durability from checkout correctness.
weak: Assumes a DB transaction covers the whole checkout.

whyNow: The baseline exposes the two core problems: cart persistence and cross-service checkout correctness.

steps.title: 2. Persistent Cart Store
steps.description: Move carts to a fast, highly-available store keyed by user (or session for guests), so they survive across devices and restarts. Carts favor availability over strict consistency, expire when abandoned, and merge a guest cart into the user's on login.
steps.decisionPrompt: A guest fills a cart, then logs in. What happens to their items, and where does the cart live?

concepts.definition: Cart writes should almost always succeed; brief inconsistency is acceptable.
concepts.whyItMatters: A failed 'add to cart' loses a sale; correctness can be reconciled at checkout.

concepts.definition: Combining a session cart with the account cart on login.
concepts.whyItMatters: Preserves intent across the auth boundary.

options.name: Fast KV store (e.g. Redis/Dynamo), TTL, AP-leaning (default)
pros: Very fast, highly available cart ops.
TTL expires abandoned carts cheaply; scales to hundreds of millions.
cons: Eventual consistency across replicas; not the place for authoritative stock/pricing.

options.name: Cart rows in the primary SQL DB
cons: High-frequency cart writes contend with order/inventory load on the primary.
Harder to scale to huge cart volume.

traps.trap: Storing carts in the same primary DB as orders/inventory
traps.why: Cart write volume contends with correctness-critical order/inventory load.
traps.instead: Use a separate fast, available store for carts; keep the primary for orders.

traps.why: Carts are stale and AP-leaning; stock and price can change before checkout.
traps.instead: Treat cart contents as intent; re-validate price and stock at checkout.

failureDrills.scenario: A cart-store replica is briefly unavailable.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Adding to cart still mostly works.
failureDrills.mitigation: AP store with failover; occasional lost write is acceptable and recoverable at checkout.

strong: Separates the cart store from the order DB; treats cart as intent.

recap.before: Carts were volatile and on the primary path.
recap.after: Carts live in a fast, available, expiring store, separate from orders.
recap.newRisk: Cart data is eventually consistent — checkout must re-validate.

whyNow: A durable, scalable cart is the foundation; correctness happens at checkout.

steps.title: 3. Pricing & Promotions
steps.description: Compute price (taxes, discounts, coupons) by a pricing service that reads promotion rules. Prices are shown live on the cart but re-evaluated and frozen at checkout, so the shopper is charged exactly what they agreed to.
steps.decisionPrompt: A coupon expires between adding to cart and checkout. What price does the shopper pay?

concepts.definition: Lock the computed total into the order at checkout time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents surprise changes and disputes; the agreed price is honored.

deepDives.title: Where pricing happens
points: Show indicative pricing on cart view (cheap, cached promotion rules).
Re-evaluate authoritatively at checkout and freeze the line prices into the order.
Validate coupon eligibility/limits server-side at checkout, not just on display.

traps.trap: Trusting the price the client sends at checkout
traps.why: A tampered client could submit any price.
traps.instead: Recompute price server-side at checkout from current rules; never trust client totals.

traps.why: Promotions/taxes can change before checkout, charging the wrong amount.
traps.instead: Re-evaluate at checkout and freeze the result into the order.

failureDrills.scenario: A promotion is misconfigured to stack infinitely.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It doesn't produce a negative or runaway total.
failureDrills.mitigation: Server-side rule limits/caps and coupon usage checks at checkout.

strong: Recomputes and freezes price server-side at checkout; validates coupons.

recap.after: A pricing service computes totals, re-validated and frozen at checkout.
recap.newRisk: Promotion rules are complex and must be capped/validated server-side.

whyNow: The authoritative total must be settled before money moves in checkout.

steps.title: 4. Inventory Reservation & Oversell Prevention
steps.description: Make 'never oversell' a hard invariant. At checkout, reserve each item with an atomic decrement-if-available (optimistic CAS) creating a short-lived reservation; the reservation expires if checkout doesn't complete. Inventory is the authoritative source of truth.
steps.decisionPrompt: The last unit of a hot item is in two shoppers' checkouts at once. How do you ensure only one gets it?

concepts.definition: A temporary hold on stock during checkout that auto-expires.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents oversell while a checkout is in flight without permanently locking stock.

concepts.definition: Atomically reduce stock only if enough remains.
concepts.whyItMatters: Concurrent checkouts can't both claim the last unit.

flows.name: Two checkouts race for the last unit
flows.note: Atomic CAS reserves for exactly one; the other gets out-of-stock.

traps.trap: Read stock, then write stock-1 as separate steps
traps.why: Two checkouts both read 1 and both write 0 — oversell.
traps.instead: Atomic decrement-if-available (CAS / conditional update); reject when it would go negative.

traps.trap: Decrementing real stock before payment succeeds
traps.why: Abandoned/failed checkouts permanently shrink available stock.
traps.instead: Reserve (separate from sold) with a TTL; convert to sold on payment, release on failure/expiry.

failureDrills.scenario: Checkout reserves stock then crashes before paying.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The reserved unit returns to available shortly.
failureDrills.mitigation: Reservation TTL expires and releases; the order saga compensates.

strong: Uses atomic CAS reservations with TTL; separates reserved from sold.

recap.after: Atomic reservations with TTL make oversell impossible while checkout is in flight.
recap.newRisk: Reservations must be released on failure/expiry — handled by the saga.

whyNow: Inventory correctness must be solid before orchestrating the full checkout.

steps.title: 5. Checkout as a Saga (idempotent)
steps.description: Orchestrate checkout as a saga: reserve inventory -> charge payment -> create order, with compensations on failure (release reservation, refund). An idempotency key makes the whole flow safe to retry, so a shopper is never double-charged or double-ordered.
steps.decisionPrompt: Payment succeeds but order creation fails. What happens to the money and the reserved stock?

concepts.definition: A coordinator runs each step and triggers compensations if a later step fails.
concepts.whyItMatters: Gives cross-service 'all-or-nothing' behavior without a distributed transaction.

concepts.definition: Undo actions (refund, release reservation) for completed steps when the saga aborts.

flows.name: Payment ok but order-create fails -> compensate
flows.note: Refund and release so no money or stock is stranded.

traps.trap: No compensation when a later step fails
traps.why: Payment without an order strands the shopper's money and the reserved stock.
traps.instead: On failure, run compensations: refund the charge and release the reservation.

traps.why: A retried request re-reserves, re-charges, or re-orders.
traps.instead: Idempotency key: a repeat returns the original result without re-running steps.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Exactly one order and one charge.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotency key dedupes the second request to the same in-progress/completed result.

strong: Orchestrates with compensations and full idempotency.

recap.before: Multi-service checkout couldn't be atomic or safely retried.
recap.after: A compensating, idempotent saga gives all-or-nothing checkout.
recap.newRisk: Saga + compensation logic is complex; every step must be idempotent.

whyNow: The saga ties inventory, payment, and orders into a correct checkout.

steps.title: 6. Order State Machine & Fulfillment
steps.description: Model the order as a state machine (placed -> paid -> fulfilled / cancelled) with guarded transitions. State changes emit events via a transactional outbox to fulfillment, notifications, and analytics — reliably and without dual writes.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you reliably tell the warehouse to ship, exactly once, when an order is paid?

concepts.definition: Only legal state changes are allowed (e.g. can't fulfill a cancelled order).
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the order lifecycle consistent and auditable.

concepts.definition: Events written in the same transaction as the state change, relayed to a queue.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees a 'paid' event is published iff the state committed — no lost or phantom ships.

options.name: Transactional outbox + queue (default)
pros: Exactly-once-effect events relative to the state change.
cons: Outbox relay + pruning to operate; consumers must be idempotent.

options.name: Directly call fulfillment from checkout
cons: A dual-write/failure leaves order state and fulfillment inconsistent (ships with no order, or order with no ship).

traps.trap: Calling fulfillment directly and writing order state separately
traps.why: A crash between the two ships without an order or orders without shipping — a dual-write hazard.
traps.instead: Write state + event in one transaction (outbox); relay to fulfillment, which dedupes.

traps.why: Cancelling a shipped order or fulfilling a cancelled one corrupts the lifecycle.

failureDrills.scenario: The fulfillment consumer redelivers a 'paid' event.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent fulfillment keyed by order id; the outbox guarantees the event matches committed state.

strong: Uses a guarded state machine + outbox; idempotent consumers.

recap.before: Order state and downstream actions could diverge.
recap.after: A guarded state machine emits reliable events via outbox to fulfillment.
recap.newRisk: Outbox relay and idempotent consumers are now required.

whyNow: A placed order must drive fulfillment reliably without dual-write bugs.

steps.title: 7. Scaling Carts vs Orders
steps.description: Scale the two workloads separately: carts (huge, read/write-heavy, availability-first) shard across a fast KV store; orders (fewer, correctness-critical) stay on a consistent, replicated store. A router directs cart traffic by user; orders scale by partitioning on order/user id.
steps.decisionPrompt: Carts get 25x the traffic of checkouts but need weaker guarantees. Why scale them differently?

concepts.definition: Carts and orders are different systems with opposite priorities (AP vs CP).
concepts.whyItMatters: One datastore/consistency model can't serve both well.

concepts.definition: Partition cart and order data by a key that keeps a user's data together.
concepts.whyItMatters: Scales horizontally while keeping a user's cart/orders local.

options.name: Sharded AP cart store + consistent order DB (default)
pros: Each workload scales on its own terms; cart volume can't threaten order correctness.
Cart KV shards by user; orders stay strongly consistent.
cons: Two systems to operate; checkout bridges them (re-validating at the boundary).

options.name: One shared SQL DB for carts and orders
pros: Simplest; one system, strong consistency everywhere.
cons: Cart write volume contends with and can starve correctness-critical order/inventory work.

deepDives.title: Bridging the two stores
points: Cart is intent (AP); at checkout, re-read authoritative price and reserve authoritative stock (CP).
Shard carts by user for locality; expire abandoned carts to bound storage.
Hot inventory items still concentrate contention — that's handled by CAS reservations, not sharding.

traps.trap: Forcing carts and orders into one consistency model
traps.why: Strong consistency for carts wastes availability/scale; weak consistency for orders risks money/inventory errors.
traps.instead: Split them: AP cart store, CP order/inventory store; reconcile at checkout.

traps.trap: Sharding orders by something other than order/user
traps.why: Scatters a user's orders and complicates lookups and the saga.
traps.instead: Partition by order/user id to keep related data and the saga local.

strong: Separates AP carts from CP orders and shards each appropriately.
Bridges them with re-validation at checkout.
weak: One shared DB and consistency model.
Lets cart load threaten order correctness.

recap.before: One system tried to serve both workloads.
recap.after: Carts and orders scale independently with fitting consistency models.
recap.newRisk: Two systems to operate; the checkout boundary must re-validate.

whyNow: The two workloads have opposite needs — separating them is the key scaling decision.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: carts live in a sharded, available, expiring KV store (intent only); a pricing service computes promotions, re-validated and frozen at checkout; checkout runs as an idempotent saga that reserves inventory via atomic CAS holds with TTL, charges payment, and creates an order, compensating on any failure; the order is a guarded state machine emitting events via a transactional outbox to fulfillment; carts (AP) and orders/inventory (CP) scale as separate systems.

functional.how: Cart service over a fast KV store.
Durable cart store keyed by user; guest merge on login.
Pricing service + rule engine, frozen at checkout.
Idempotent saga: reserve -> charge -> create order.
Guarded order state machine with compensations.

nonFunctional.requirement: Never oversell / never charge without order

nonFunctional.how: Idempotency key dedupes retries to one order/charge.
Sharded AP KV store separate from orders.
Consistent order DB + saga + outbox events.
Server-side re-evaluation and price freeze at checkout.
AP cart shards + CP order/inventory store.

say: Restate it: a durable cart and a correct, multi-service checkout (payment + inventory + order).
List functional (cart CRUD, pricing, checkout, lifecycle) and non-functional (no oversell, idempotent checkout, cart availability, order durability).
Name the split: carts are availability-first; orders/inventory are correctness-first.
Note cart ops dwarf checkouts but need weaker guarantees.
Sketch cart item PUT, cart GET, idempotent checkout, and cancel.
Start naive to expose the distributed-transaction problem, then a persistent cart store, then pricing.
Add inventory reservation/CAS, then the idempotent checkout saga, then the order state machine + outbox.
Go deep where pushed: the saga + compensations, or oversell prevention.
Scale carts (AP, sharded) and orders (CP) separately; bridge at checkout.
Summarize requirements and name the dominant risk (cross-service checkout correctness).

expectations: Persists the cart instead of keeping it in memory.
Knows checkout must reserve stock and take payment, and that retries shouldn't double-charge.
Designs an idempotent checkout saga with compensations across services.
Prevents oversell with atomic CAS reservations + TTL.
Re-validates and freezes price server-side at checkout.
Splits AP carts from CP orders/inventory and scales each appropriately.
Uses a guarded order state machine + transactional outbox for reliable fulfillment.
Reasons through every partial-failure branch and its compensation.

followUps: How would you handle partial fulfillment and split shipments from multiple warehouses?
How do you support returns and refunds that reverse inventory and payment after fulfillment?
How would you implement a flash sale where one SKU sees extreme checkout contention?
How do you reconcile inventory across the reservation store, orders, and the warehouse system of record?
How would you support saved carts, wishlists, and 'buy now' (skip-cart) flows?
How do you prevent and detect payment fraud during checkout without hurting conversion?
